title: Underwater Suit-Wearing Cyborg Insect Capable of Hours-Long Diving and Terra-Aqua Travel
abstract: Researchers built a wearable diving suit for a Madagascar hissing cockroach, turning a land insect into an amphibious cyborg robot. A miniaturised oxygen generator (H2O2 + MnO2 catalyst, no electronics) feeds oxygen to the insect's thoracic spiracles through a waterproof flexible shell, sustaining respiration and controlled locomotion underwater for up to 3 hours. The suit-wearing cyborg hit 1.0 body-lengths/sec underwater, outpacing most amphibious robots, and traversed a mixed CO2-and-water hazard tunnel plus a 2 cm underwater crevice with a fully implanted controller. Nature Communications, open access, published 29 June 2026.
user_notes: High-visibility journal paper (60k accesses, 958 Altmetric already). Lead with the striking image. Honest angle: this is a clever hardware and chemistry result for search-and-rescue in flooded spaces, but it is a biohybrid cyborg using a living insect, so note the ethics/where-it-fits question that will drive replies. The oxygen comes from a catalytic reaction, not a battery, which is the genuinely neat engineering bit.

title: SwarmForage: Emergent Division of Labor in 1000-Robot Swarms
